title: How to break the memory supercycle? Or is it even possible?
gist: A detailed bottom-up model (prompted by SK Group's claim that customers want 4-5x more DRAM supply but wafer supply will only ~2x by 2030) forecasts DRAM/HBM capacity growing from ~2M to ~4.8M WPM by 2030 (up to 5.68M if China is unconstrained), against aggregate demand of ~157.5 EB versus roughly 91-120 EB of realistic supply, implying a persistent ~25% general DRAM deficit and a total 2030 DRAM market size of $2.6-3.26 trillion (HBM alone $1.5-1.8T), with China potentially entering the Western server DRAM supply chain as Big 3 capacity increasingly shifts to HBM.

insights.claim: The author models the 'Big 4' DRAM makers (Samsung, SK Hynix, Micron, CXMT) growing combined DRAM capacity from around 2M wafers-per-month (WPM) at end-2026 to about 4.8M WPM by 2030 in the base case, or as high as 5.68M WPM if Chinese expansion (via CXMT) is completely unconstrained and its HBM roadmap executes well.
insights.speaker: M
insights.so_what: A base-case 2.4x capacity increase by 2030 (2M to 4.8M WPM) is the central supply-side input for modeling whether DRAM/HBM shortages persist through the decade — the single most important variable for AI accelerator cost and availability forecasts.
insights.quote: By the end of 2026, the Big 4 will have around 2M WPM of DRAM capacity. I expect this to grow to 4.8M WPM by 2030

insights.claim: China's memory expansion is modeled as the single largest source of capacity growth uncertainty because Chinese cleanroom construction takes about 12 months versus 21-24 months elsewhere, capital is not a constraint given state and bank financing, but litho tool availability under potential US export restrictions (the MATCH Act) is the key swing factor that could derail CXMT and China's expansion plans.
insights.speaker: M
insights.so_what: Identifies litho export policy (not capital or construction time) as the binding constraint on Chinese memory capacity growth — a specific geopolitical lever that could swing global DRAM supply by hundreds of thousands of WPM depending on how the MATCH Act plays out.
insights.quote: Frankly, the biggest constraint for China is the availability of litho tools when it comes to scaling capacity. If the MATCH Act passes and DUV sales are banned, then it will derail CXMT's & China's memory expansion plans.

insights.claim: SMEE's domestic DUVi lithography tool was shipped to CXMT and SMIC with beta testing nearly complete and mass production beginning late 2026 or early 2027, while Yuliangsheng/SiCarrier's DUVi is expected to enter mass production in 2028, suggesting lithography is unlikely to constrain Chinese logic (5nm/7nm+) and memory production after 2028 absent new export bans.
insights.speaker: M
insights.so_what: A specific 2027-2028 timeline for domestic Chinese DUV lithography tools reaching mass production is a key marker for when China's semiconductor self-sufficiency in mature lithography becomes largely immune to further US export restrictions.
insights.quote: SMEE DUVi was shipped to CXMT & SMIC last year, and beta testing is nearly over, with mass production beginning from late 2026 or early 2027. Yuliangsheng/SiCarrier's DUVi is expected to enter mass production in 2028.

insights.claim: Samsung and SK Hynix's planned US investments are modeled as focused on HBM packaging rather than new memory fabs, with HBM wafers flowing in from Korea for stacking at US packaging facilities before shipment to TSMC's Arizona facility, likely in exchange for tariff exemptions on semiconductors for Korea.
insights.speaker: M
insights.so_what: If correct, this implies Korean memory makers' US investments will not add net new wafer-fab DRAM capacity, meaning US onshoring narratives for memory should be read as packaging/assembly reshoring rather than fab capacity reshoring.
insights.quote: I personally believe that their US investments will be focused on HBM packaging rather than memory fabs in exchange for no tariffs on semiconductors for Korea.

themes.theme: AI-driven demand outstrips even bullish supply growth

insights.claim: The model estimates aggregate 2030 DRAM-equivalent demand of about 157.5 exabytes: 75 EB from agentic AI CPUs (assumed 1:1 CPU:GPU ratio with 2.5TB DDR6 per CPU), 25 EB from general cloud/IaaS CPUs, 22.5 EB from HBM5-equipped accelerators, 15 EB from HBM4e-equipped accelerators, and 20 EB from consumer DRAM — explicitly excluding any demand from physical AI (humanoids, autonomous cars).
insights.speaker: M
insights.so_what: A granular demand breakdown showing agentic-AI CPU memory (75 EB) as the single largest demand driver — larger than HBM itself — reframes the memory shortage narrative: it is not just an HBM/accelerator story but increasingly a general-purpose AI-server DRAM story.
insights.quote: So we have an aggregate demand of 157.5 EB (75EB + 25EB + 22.5EB + 15EB + 20EB) by 2030.

insights.claim: Accelerator unit sales are expected to hit 30 million units by 2030, split between roughly 15 million units averaging 1TB of HBM4e and 15 million units averaging 1.5TB of HBM5, implying total accelerator HBM demand of 15 EB (HBM4e) plus 22.5 EB (HBM5).
insights.speaker: M
insights.so_what: A specific 30M-unit 2030 AI accelerator shipment forecast with per-unit HBM capacity assumptions is a concrete, testable input for sizing the HBM total addressable market and for cross-checking against vendor (Nvidia, AMD, hyperscaler ASIC) shipment guidance as it emerges.
insights.quote: Accelerator sales are expected to hit 30M units by 2030. I expect 15M accelerators to have an average of 1TB of HBM4e and another 15M accelerators to have an average of 1.5TB of HBM5.

insights.claim: HBM dies require roughly a 4x multiplier in effective DRAM wafer capacity versus equal-capacity DDR5 dies (up from about 2.7x for HBM3E) because HBM dies are 35-45% larger due to TSVs and ultra-wide I/Os, fewer dies fit per wafer, and TSV processing plus stacking heavily reduce net yield, with the multiplier growing further for HBM5 as interface width doubles from 2048 to 4096.
insights.speaker: M
insights.so_what: The ~4x wafer-capacity 'tax' for HBM production versus standard DRAM is the core mechanical reason why memory makers' pivot to HBM is starving the general DRAM market of supply — a critical mechanism explaining simultaneous HBM and general DRAM price spikes.
insights.quote: around 2.7x more effective DRAM wafer capacity is needed to deliver the same number of good memory bits for HBM3E. This multiplier grows to 4x for HBM4E and even more for HBM5 as the interface width doubles from 2048 to 4096.

themes.theme: Structural deficit and market sizing to 2030

insights.claim: The model calculates roughly 91.31 EB/year of general DRAM supply by 2030 against about 120 EB/year of DRAM demand (ex-HBM), a 28.69 EB deficit, though accelerated Chinese capacity expansion (an incremental 860k WPM) could narrow this to a 1.19 EB deficit while adding roughly 7 EB/year of Chinese HBM supply.
insights.speaker: M
insights.so_what: Quantifies a persistent structural DRAM deficit (~25-29 EB/year in the base case) even after all announced capacity additions are realized, directly supporting a thesis of sustained elevated DRAM pricing through 2030 absent an unexpected demand slowdown.
insights.quote: we get around 91.31 EB/year of general DRAM supply compared to 120EB/year of DRAM demand.

insights.claim: The author expects a roughly 25% deficit in the general DRAM market (91 EB/year supply versus 120 EB/year demand) by 2030, with DRAM ASPs remaining inflated in the $1.5-2.0/Gb range and HBM pricing reaching $5-6/Gb, implying a total 2030 DRAM market size of $2.60-3.26 trillion (midpoint $2.93 trillion), split roughly $1.10-1.46 trillion general DRAM and $1.50-1.80 trillion HBM.
insights.speaker: M
insights.so_what: A $2.6-3.26 trillion total 2030 DRAM market size estimate (with HBM alone reaching $1.5-1.8T) is a striking bull case for memory-maker equity valuations (Samsung, SK Hynix, Micron) if the underlying deficit assumptions hold — a headline number worth stress-testing against consensus sell-side memory TAM estimates.
insights.quote: the general DRAM market size will be around $1.10T-$1.46T and the HBM market will be around $1.50T-$1.80T. The total DRAM market will be around $2.60T-$3.26T (midpoint $2.93T).

insights.claim: Because roughly 70% of the Big 3's wafer capacity will go toward serving HBM demand (unless they expand faster than modeled), the author argues China will enter the Western server DRAM supply chain as a structural consequence of Korean/US memory makers reallocating capacity toward HBM.
insights.speaker: M
insights.so_what: A contrarian implication: even without policy changes, the HBM boom itself could force Western server OEMs to source general-purpose DRAM from Chinese suppliers (CXMT, YMTC/XMC) by the end of the decade — a geopolitically sensitive supply-chain shift worth monitoring for both national-security and investment implications.
insights.quote: China will also enter the Western server DRAM supply chain as the vast majority of the Big 3's wafer capacity (70%) will go towards serving HBM demand unless they expand faster.

insights.claim: The analysis explicitly excludes any DRAM demand from physical AI (humanoid robots and autonomous vehicles), noting that even if the modeled data-center and AI demand estimates prove too high, that gap would likely be offset by unmodeled physical-AI memory demand.
insights.speaker: M
insights.so_what: Flags a significant unmodeled upside demand source (humanoid robots, autonomous vehicles) that could push the memory deficit even higher than the base case suggests if physical AI adoption accelerates as expected.
insights.quote: As you can see, I haven't modeled any DRAM demand for physical AI (humanoids & autonomous cars), so even if my DC & AI demand estimates are too high (they are not), it will be balanced out by physical AI demand.

themes.theme: Corroborating context: SK Group's supply-demand gap claim

insights.claim: The analysis was prompted by a reported comment from the SK Group Chairman that customers are asking for 4x-5x more memory supply than currently available, while wafer supply is expected to only double (2x) by 2030 — a gap the author's independent model broadly corroborates through its own bottom-up supply and demand build.
insights.speaker: M
insights.so_what: An SK Group leadership acknowledgment of a 4-5x demand versus 2x supply gap is a rare direct industry-insider confirmation of the severity of the memory supply-demand imbalance, lending credibility to the broader memory-supercycle thesis.
insights.quote: A few days ago, SK Group Chairman said that customers are asking for 4x-5x more supply, but wafer supply will only double by 2x by 2030.
